title: Cyber Security Incident Identification from Correlated ESP and Endpoint Alerts
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the NERC CIP programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with NERC CIP control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-008-6 R1 Part 1.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-008-6 R1 Part 1.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.38: Cyber Security Incident Identification from Correlated ESP and Endpoint Alerts.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.38' running on Splunk ES notable macro (or index=notable), BES asset tags, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Cyber Security Incident Identification from Correlated ESP a' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper NERC CIP control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the NERC CIP control objective.

dataSources: Splunk ES `` `notable` `` macro (or `index=notable`), BES asset tags — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
`notable` earliest=-7d
| lookup bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv dest as asset OUTPUT bes_system
| where isnotnull(bes_system)
| stats earliest(_time) as detected_time values(rule_name) as rules dc(asset) as assets by urgency
| sort - detected_time
description: We group perimeter, authentication, and malware signals affecting BES assets to support timely identification of reportable cyber security incidents.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes notable. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Tag ES notables with BES context at correlation time. (2) Define which rules imply CIP-008 review. (3) Integrate with incident command workflow. (4) Preserve search artifacts per policy. Indexes required: `index=notable`; Lookups: `bes_cyber_asset_inventory`, `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv`.
visualization: Table (candidate incidents), Timeline, Single value (open BES-tagged notables). Key fields: `assets`, `detected_time`, `rules`, `urgency`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Planned generation outage maintenance** — Scheduled maintenance outages on BES Cyber Systems require physical and electronic access that triggers CIP access monitoring alerts; these are pre-approved via the outage management system and documented in the maintenance plan.

2. **NERC compliance audit field activities** — During triennial CIP compliance audits, Regional Entity auditors perform physical walkthroughs, system sampling, and configuration reviews that generate access and configuration query events.

3. **Relay and protection system testing** — Periodic relay testing, protection coordination studies, and SCADA system communication tests produce legitimate BES operational signals that are scheduled and documented in the maintenance programme.

4. **Vendor remote access for approved patches** — ICS/SCADA vendors granted time-limited interactive remote access (IRA) through the jump host for approved firmware updates and configuration changes are covered by the vendor risk management programme.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nerc_cip_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved outage tickets, audit period dates, relay test schedule entries, and vendor IRA session IDs. The search filters against this lookup and suppresses events where `cip_exception_approved=true` and the window is active.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `CIP-008-6 R1 Part 1.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-008-6 R1 Part 1.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.38: Cyber Security Incident Identification from Correlated ESP and Endpoint Alerts." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Tag ES notables with BES context at correlation time. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** Define which rules imply CIP-008 review. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Integrate with incident command workflow. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 4:** Preserve search artifacts per policy. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Cyber Security Incident Identification from Correlated ESP and Endpoint Alerts," the aggregation summarises incident flow — volume per category, time-to-contain distribution, and resolution cadence are the metrics regulators ask about during post-incident review.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` — the BES Cyber System inventory required for NERC CIP asset identification. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`bes_system`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `dest`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval dest = lower(dest)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 7 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.13.38: Cyber Security Incident Identification from Correlated ESP a]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.13.38,reg=NERC_CIP
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `assets`, `detected_time`, `rules`, `urgency`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table assets, detected_time, rules, urgency
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.13.38: NERC CIP evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`candidate incidents`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`open BES-tagged notables`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'NERC CIP current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NERC CIP-specific failure modes:**

• BES Cyber System classification (CIP-002): every NERC CIP detection must filter to BES Cyber Systems only. A general 'Windows login' search across the entire estate is not CIP evidence; a search filtered through `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` is.

• Low-Impact vs Medium-Impact vs High-Impact: CIP requirements scale with impact rating. A detection that fires identically for Low and High-Impact systems over-applies controls (wastes IR effort) and under-applies audit attention. Include impact rating in the alert payload and route by rating.

• ERC (Electronic Security Perimeter) boundary: CIP-005 requires strict ESP documentation. Firewall-rule detections must reference the ESP boundary register — a rule change on a firewall that's not an EAP is NOT a CIP finding; a rule change on an EAP is.

• CIP-010 configuration baselines: baseline monitoring must use an approved baseline, not an informal one. Reference the CIP-010 attestation register in the UC; baselines without dated attestation signatures are not regulatory evidence.
grandmaExplanation: We group perimeter, authentication, and malware signals affecting BES assets to support timely identification of reportable cyber security incidents. That is important for the big power grid, where proof needs to line up with what the rules expect.
